theory.canonical_statement: For any diffeomorphism $\phi: M \to M$ that preserves the symmetry group of $M$, $\phi(p) = p$ and $\phi$ leaves the local lemniscatic structure invariant.
theory.technical_interpretation: For any diffeomorphism $\phi: M \to M$ that preserves the symmetry group of $M$, $\phi(p) = p$ and $\phi$ leaves the local lemniscatic structure invariant.

canonical_equation.source_section_latex:
\[
\phi : M \to M,
\qquad
\phi(p)=p
\]
\[
\phi(\Lambda)=\Lambda
\]
for every symmetry-preserving diffeomorphism $\phi$ and every local lemniscatic representative $\Lambda$ through the TZP.

equation_progression.interpretive_role: This limits the discussion to the transformations the framework actually admits as physical or geometric symmetries.
This keeps the anchor from moving under reparameterization and preserves the uniqueness obtained in \tzpidref{0000}.
This closes the progression by showing that symmetry preserves not just the point but also the topological neighborhood structure that later dimensional branching uses.

external_sources.title: TZP Stability Under Symmetry-Preserving Diffeomorphisms
external_sources.canonical_claim: For any diffeomorphism $\phi: M \to M$ that preserves the symmetry group of $M$, $\phi(p) = p$ and $\phi$ leaves the local lemniscatic structure invariant.
external_sources.exact_language: This source (Reflections on the Lemniscate of Bernoulli: The Forty‑Eight Faces of a Mathematical Gem) supports the mathematical structure (symmetry of self‑intersections) by showing that The paper notes that the lemniscate has three self‑intersection nodes and that any projective symmetry of the lemniscate must permute these nodes.
external_sources.remaining_gap: While the cited source provides partial support, a dedicated mathematical or physical proof specific to the Trawinistic framework is still required to fully establish this entry.
external_sources.source_title: Reflections on the Lemniscate of Bernoulli: The Forty‑Eight Faces of a Mathematical Gem
external_sources.relevance: The paper notes that the lemniscate has three self‑intersection nodes and that any projective symmetry of the lemniscate must permute these nodes. This demonstrates that the self‑intersection pattern is preserved under symmetry‑preserving transformations, providing an analogy for diffeomorphisms that fix the zero point and leave the local lemniscatic structure invariant.
